figures.caption: Figure 1: Operator Boosting replaces a full-size monolithic neural operator with an additive stack of residual operators. Here 𝑎 denotes the input field, 𝐺0(𝑎) = 0 is the normalized-output mean initializer, and each stage contributes a validation-shrunk correction 𝜂𝑚𝐻𝑓 𝑚 (𝑎) to form the boosted prediction.

Figure 2: Accuracy–parameter Pareto plane normalized by the best full-size baseline for each PDE. Points below and to the left of (1, 1) empirically Pareto-dominate the best full-size baseline. Representative boosted stacks show both strong Pareto improvements and compression tradeoffs.

Figure 3: One stage of same-family Operator Boosting. Each stage fits the current residual field using a tiny operator from the fixed family 𝑓 , selects a scalar shrinkage coefficient on validation data, and adds the shrunken correction to the current stack.

Figure 4: Aggregate summary of the completed homogeneous Operator Boosting experiments. Across 30 dataset– family pairs, 21 have positive mean accuracy gains and 17 have completely positive confidence intervals. In best-model comparisons, boosted stacks empirically Pareto-dominate the best full-size baseline on 7 of 10 PDE benchmarks.
Table 1 Within-family accuracy–parameter tradeoffs grouped by architecture family. Positive Δperf indicates lower test relative 𝐿2 error for the boosted tiny stack than for the full-size baseline.

Table 2 Best full-size baseline versus its boosted tiny stack. For each dataset, the best full-size architecture is selected by mean test relative 𝐿2 error and compared with the boosted tiny stack from the same family.

Table 3 Best full-size baseline versus best boosted tiny stack. For each dataset, the best full-size baseline and best boosted tiny surrogate are selected by mean test relative 𝐿2 error. This comparison permits architecture selection among completed boosted stacks.
